title: Structural responses incipient to pressure-driven antiferromagnetic quantum critical point of van der Waals heavy-fermion metal CeSiI
authors: Hanming Ma, Tong Shi, Wenhao Li, Qingxin Dong, Xiaoli Ma, Shaoheng Ruan, Zhongjin Wu, Pengtao Yang, Zhaoming Tian, Jianping Sun, Yoshiya Uwatoko, Xiaohui Yu, Hechang Lei, Bosen Wang, Jinguang Cheng
abstract: CeSiI is a van der Waals heavy-fermion metal recently found to exhibit unconventional superconductivity near a pressure-induced antiferromagnetic quantum critical point (QCP) at Pc =6 GPa. Here, we report a comprehensive single-crystal X-ray diffraction study of CeSiI under high pressures up to 8.3 GPa at room temperature, revealing subtle structural responses that precede pressure-driven QCP. We find that the unit-cell volume decreases smoothly upon compression without showing any structural phase transition in the investigated pressure range. Intriguingly, we observe abrupt and concurrent anisotropic responses of the lattice parameters around Pc =6 GPa, i.e., the a-axis contracts while the c-axis enlongated suddenly, with the unit-cell volume smoothily varies with pressure. Structural refinements further show that these lattice anomalies primarily originate from changes of Ce-Ce and Ce-Si bond lengths, as well as a flattening of the inner honeycomb Si layer within the CeSiI monolayer around Pc. Our findings establish an interesting case linking pressure-driven electronic transition of QCP at low temperatures to incipient structural responses at room temperature, thereby providing fresh insight into the pressure-temperature phase diagram of CeSiI.
summary: This work uses high-pressure single-crystal X-ray diffraction to investigate the structure of the heavy-fermion metal CeSiI near its antiferromagnetic Quantum Critical Point. The researchers found that while the overall crystal structure remains stable, abrupt anisotropic changes in lattice parameters occur around the critical pressure. These structural anomalies are linked to local bond length and coordination symmetry changes within the material's layers.
main_problem: To understand the structural responses that precede a pressure-driven antiferromagnetic quantum critical point (QCP) in the van der Waals heavy-fermion metal CeSiI.
main_result: The study found abrupt and concurrent anisotropic lattice parameter changes near the QCP, which are attributed to local structural deformations like flattening of the Si honeycomb layer and changes in Ce-Ce/Ce-Si bond lengths.
method: Comprehensive single-crystal X-ray diffraction (SXRD) measurements were performed on high-quality single crystals under pressures up to 8.3 GPa at room temperature.
model_or_system: The physical system is CeSiI, a van der Waals heavy-fermion metal known for unconventional superconductivity near an AF QCP. The structure involves layered components: a Si honeycomb layer sandwiched between Ce and I layers.
key_observables: Unit-cell volume ($V$), lattice parameters ($a$, $c$), interlayer gaps ($	ext{h}_{	ext{I-I}}^{	ext{Inter}}$), intra-layer distances ($	ext{h}_{	ext{Ce-Ce}}$, $	ext{h}_{	ext{Si-Si}}$), and bond angles/lengths.
important_parameters: Pressure range up to 8.3 GPa; critical pressure $P_c \approx 6$ GPa; room temperature measurements.
main_assumptions: The structural anomalies observed at room temperature are considered incipient precursors to the low-temperature electronic QCP.
figures_summary: Figures show SXRD patterns, pressure dependence of lattice parameters ($a, c, V$), and detailed evolution plots for various interatomic distances and bond angles across the critical pressure region.
paper_structure: The paper presents systematic high-pressure SXRD data analysis, first establishing the overall structural stability (no phase transition) while highlighting anomalies in lattice parameters near $P_c$. It then delves into specific atomic interactions (bond lengths, layer heights) to microscopically explain these observed macroscopic structural changes.
why_interesting: The direct experimental link established between measurable room-temperature structural precursors and a low-temperature electronic QCP provides crucial input for theories describing quantum critical points in correlated materials.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks
